item.title: super good
item.content:encoded: this is one of the best SCPs I've had the pleasure of reading

item.title: Re: Question
item.content:encoded:
This has been up for over a year now and no one has noticed this before ¬_¬
I'll fix it, thanks!

item.title: Question
item.content:encoded:
is it correct?

Commentator 1: That's what I love about this game. You never know where the next surprise is going to come from. Of course, Player 2's options are somewhat limited now that his hand has become a giant crab claw.

Commentator 1: You're right about that one, Commentator 2!

i think this is proper

Commentator 1: That's what I love about this game. You never know where the next surprise is going to come from.

Commentator 2: Of course, Player 2's options are somewhat limited now that his hand has become a giant crab claw.

Commentator 1: You're right about that one, Commentator 2!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, that was amazing.

Unknown. Was observed to beat rock.

This would be my favorite part, except I think the whole thing might be my favorite part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was an instant favorite. Ftagn!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The commentators not calling anyone by name is what got me at the end. It makes it feel like something with a slight grasp of humanity trying to fit in.

Question: could they get someone who transformed to write down the full rules to the game, or would that be a cognitohazard?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unknown. Was observed to beat rock.

Upvoted. Giving it eight thumbs up for each arm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wanna upvote but the count is 231 so I’ll wait.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's been allowed, just that its not commonly used thing nowadays. I don't know for certain though. Considering the article has remained up for this long though, I think it's fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomaly Class

Is that acceptable now? I haven't been on for a while, so I'm just making sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad someone got the reference, heh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with some of the other comments that said all of the gestures in the table needed an explanation, I thought it was clear enough that the foundation had multiple records of 4633 occurring.
Overall, it has a nice old-school short form feel to it
+1
(P.S. I see that eternal darkness reference in the incident log, you sly dog)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nah, the gestures they throw are anatomically correct. The kids used to say "Tsoo-yea-fah" when throwing the gestures, though.
Now that I think of it, the phrase kinda fits the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't this how russians play rock paper scissors?

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Orkh-ka-no-lēsa? It's not possible! Nobody has ever been able to throw him!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just labelled every throw with [number] cuz I can't name them.

+ Another match from the 2010 championship
- My arch rival, it was our destiny to meet here on the battlefield.

Round 1
P1 preemptively declares rock.
P1[rock]
P2[1] fingers stretch to become webbed: willingly lose current round and gain ability to photosynthesize to gain energy to execute more extreme hand mutations. Current weather is sunny.
P1 declares to use a move that changes weather.

Round 2
P1[2] all fingers bend backwards and twists and crawls up the arm, several cuts form from the hand up to the shoulder: covers body in layer of previous round's throw: rock. will affect next round's throw too, if next round's throw loses to previous round's throw.
P2[Ill-arl-teth]: P2 predicts P1 and uses coral hand to begin chipping down opponent's rock armor

Round 3
P1[scissors]: P1 now covered in scissors and rock.
P2[3] hand twists in on itself in an infinite fractal: sends the next throw back in time.
P1 body slams the coral. Coral is intact due to extra energy from photosynthesis. Also, coral beats scissors. As well as rock.

Round 4
P1[4] hand detaches and flies up, forming eldritch, fist-sized portal with index and ring fingers, while blood pouring from the wrist forms a pentacle: creates P1's throw from Round n-1 in the air to collide into P2's throw from Round n-2 at high speed on the next turn, if latter is still in effect.
P2[5] hand grows many fingers that connect to each other and start flashing: ability to read minds. P2 places [5] hand into [3] hand. Both hands are now occupied.

Round 5
P1[6] hand grows and forms chitin: shields self. mind read is blocked. unable to move on the next turn. P1 declares to do nothing on next round. P1 then declares to do something on next round.
P2[7]: grow one large arm. P2 is now out of energy.

P2 declares he will throw rock or Sha-nak-nar and [8]. P1's trainer, unable to communicate verbally, asks for clarification on whether the AND or OR is precedent in the declaration entirely using Daevite sign language (a sign language with no anomalous properties). Everyone looks confused at his flailing about. The trainer is kicked out of the tournament venue by the presiding official. P1 verbally repeats the question, P2 does not respond. P1 pretends to start sweating visibly to put P2 into a false sense of security.

The round ends and the scissors are now flung towards P2's coral hand, slicing it off cleanly. P2 swiftly takes out a first-aid kit hidden in his backpack and treats his amputated arm.

Round 6
P1 does nothing this round.
P2[scissors]: P2 wins. P1 and P2 are now tied. P2 declares rock next round.

Round 7
P1[spock]
P2[Texas Longhorn]
Both players penalized for using illegal gestures.

Sorry, I just googled up Rock Paper Scissors League and I had things to say about those tournament rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quick question, if I want to do the Rah-kesh-et, do I need my thumb to extend through my whole arm, torso, and other arm, before it emerges from my opposing thumbnail? I don't know if I should start learning how to tunnel my thumb through human flesh, or if that will be redundant to simply creating a portal on the palm of my hand.
Oh my, he's done it! He's used the Orkh-ka-no-lēsa move which forces the other player's hand to upvote SCP-4633!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I agree that most of the above crit sounds like pedantic nitpicking, I do think there's merit to the point that the article doesn't mention how players and spectators anomalously pick up on the new rules, and I don't think that would bloat the article terribly.

That said, it's always possible that the same mind-affecting properties keep the Foundation from noticing this detail, so I disagree that the omission breaks the internal logic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, but superb. What a fun concept.

item.title: Nice read
item.content:encoded: That was a pretty fun read. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Over 4000 SCPs in and this community is still coming up with fantastically creative and unique article ideas. This hits pretty much all the right buttons for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's implied that the email contains this information, or at least conveys it - it says that's where it was all discovered in the first place, and emails typically don't just go to a single Foundation email address. The table says that it has examples of the gestures they are aware of, implying that they are aware of many more.

If it's your requirement that articles explain in exacting detail the source of every single piece of information the Foundation has, the length of every article on the site would double - this kind of informational assumption is pretty standard across the whole wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, let me explain. The Foundation describes seven1 gestures related to SCP-4633 in the first addendum. The image in the email only showed three of these anomalous gestures, and the other four aren’t featured in the recording of Incident 4633-1. So, how did the Foundation learn to perform them? As far as the article tells us, the email and the incident are the only sources of information about SCP-4633. And making the D-Class move their hands in millions of random combinations until they hit gold doesn’t seem plausible.

This is a plot hole. There’s no in-universe explanation on how the Foundation could have discovered these movements or their names. In this discussion thread, you explain that the email grants knowledge anomalously – but this isn’t mentioned in the actual article, so the flaw persists in the original work.

For the same reason, it’s unclear how it’s known which movements win against which. You mention they “just observe” what the players say, but the article doesn’t give this impression: it’s not stated at any point that players understand anomalously the rules of the game. The only anomalies described are that 1) people move hand, 2) people get tentacle, 3) people not mad about it; nothing suggests that subjects acquire knowledge or master the game. So it’s hard to figure out what’s telling the Foundation that X sign wins against Y.

I think the internal logic of the article could be improved if you added a couple of lines to address these issues: saying that the emailed text describes several hand movements (or adding more extra extremities to the photo that show additional gestures) and describing the behavior of the players during the matches should be enough.

And that’s my opinion X-D. Either way, I repeat that this article seems incredibly well-executed to me, and I don’t think the flaws in internal logic ruin the alien feeling that you’ve conveyed here. I’m downvoting because I place a lot of value in consistency.

Footnotes
1. Not six; my bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't even know what you are talking about with these six gestures. I don't feel that it's necessary that the foundation learned about things through testing - I kind of think thats a given for like 90% of the articles on the entire site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's no mention in the article that subjects engaging in SCP-4633 games instinctively know about its rules, either. And there's still no intradiegetical explanation on how there are registers about six gestures.

My point remains the same — there are significant holes on how the Foundation knows all these things, and they're never addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, there's no explanation on how the Foundation knows the names and gestures of six movements1, nor how they deduce a game is "won" when they test them.

Simple observation. They watch the people who have been affected and write down what they say and what happens. This isn't some grand mystery or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is incredibly compelling and well-executed, and the picture acted as a strong hook for me. However, there's no explanation on how the Foundation knows the names and gestures of six movements1, nor how they deduce a game is "won" when they test them. Subjectively, I don't feel the logs escalate as much or establish as strong of an atmosphere as, I think, an article like this requires. It's a -1 for now.

On the other hand, you've made me search the US Rock Paper Scissors League in Google, and O_O

Footnotes
1. You explain it in this discussion, but it remains a plothole within the actual article.

item.title: 40th doot
item.content:encoded: Very fun and, at the same time, disturbing thing. Especially the log, really hits me with that "something isn't right" feeling. The image looks nice too. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: revenge for ruining… err… whatever it was you did

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God fucking dammit Mortos

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I guess I got pretty lucky in that the actually commentary for competitive Rock Paper Scissors is exactly as absurd as it is in this article, heh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks!
The idea behind the discovery is that the email was probably anomalous, given how it was able to communicate anomalous ideas like this. But yeah, it's not meant to be super flashy or anything, just a small link in an ongoing chain of… something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. You have a great, absurd premise that you deliver on the potential of. The sense of progression in the broadcast was excellent. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You seem to have something for body squick, and it's always done well.

My only issue with SCP-4633 is probably its mundane1 discovery, but I understand that you may have been told that it was necessary for cohesive purposes.

+1

Footnotes
1. In my opinion.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This one started off as a single-line concept in my head, "rock paper fhtagn", and went through a couple of iterations before I settled on this one.

Yes, the USA Rock Paper Scissors League was a real thing. Yes, it had commentary, it was on ESPN. Yes, the commentary was almost exactly like this.

Thanks to Rounderhouse, TheMightyMcB, Lazar, weryllium, MaliceAF, Hippo and Tufto for reading the draft of this!

Check out more of my stuff here!

Image creds:
Image is an edited composite of 2 other images -
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/Rock,_Paper,_Scissors#/media/File:Mushi-ken_(%E8%99%AB%E6%8B%B3),_Japanese_rock-paper-scissors_variant,_from_the_Kensarae_sumai_zue_(1809).jpg

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hagfish#/media/File:NIE_1905_Hagfish_-_Myxine_glutinosa.jpg
